teachingPoint: Return eventId at storage. Everything after EventCreated is async — organizer does not wait for audience scoring or notifications.
positiveExample: NODE A2 stores event, emits EventCreated, returns { eventId } synchronously. B/C/T62 all async.
negativeExample: Organizer waits for search indexing or audience matching before receiving eventId — unusable UX.
discriminatingConstraint: Synchronous response boundary ends at storage — not at completion of downstream async operations.
keywords: eventId storage Everything EventCreated async organizer wait audience scoring notifications

teachingPoint: T62 Campaign Analytics is time-decoupled from NODE D completion gate. EventPromotionCompleted fires when promotion pipeline completes (minutes). PromotionCampaignCompleted fires after 7-day attribution window. Never couple them.
positiveExample: T62 triggers on EventCreated independently. NODE D gate: A2+B2+C1+C2. T62 NOT in gate.
negativeExample: Include T62 in NODE D gate — EventPromotionCompleted fires 7 days later. FLOW-08 receives nothing during promotion window. No local error.
discriminatingConstraint: When two processes have different time scales, they must have different completion events.
keywords: Campaign Analytics time-decoupled completion gate EventPromotionCompleted fires promotion pipeline completes minutes PromotionCampaignCompleted

teachingPoint: Feed injection uses F201 IFeedInjectionService (WRITE interface), not FLOW-02's F188/F189 (READ interfaces). Same domain, different operations, different scaling profiles.
positiveExample: C1 declares dependency on F201 IFeedInjectionService. CQRS: read and write are separate interfaces.
negativeExample: Reuse existing feed service from FLOW-02 — CQRS violation. Read interface cannot handle mass writes. Fails silently under load.
discriminatingConstraint: When read and write serve the same domain, they require separate interfaces with separate scaling profiles.
keywords: Feed injection uses F201 IFeedInjectionService WRITE interface FLOW-02 F188 F189 READ interfaces

teachingPoint: Notification channel selection is a platform trust boundary, not an organizer preference. Channel rules are MACHINE. Wording is FREEDOM. An organizer who can select SMS for all users at score 0.1 can flood the platform.
positiveExample: C2 channel rules MACHINE: IN_APP=always, PUSH=score>=0.6, EMAIL=score>=0.4, SMS=urgent+score>=0.8+50km.
negativeExample: Let organizer select push, email, SMS for their event — platform trust boundary violated.
discriminatingConstraint: Channel selection is enforcement, not configuration.
keywords: Notification channel selection platform trust boundary organizer preference rules MACHINE Wording FREEDOM

teachingPoint: Audience scoring reads from xiigen-matching-profiles (GLOBAL index written by FLOW-02 A1). Never reads from xiigen-business-profiles (PRIVATE). Four public-safe fields only.
positiveExample: B1 reads from xiigen-matching-profiles GLOBAL. Security arbiter BLOCKS any read from PRIVATE profiles.
negativeExample: Score users using their full business profiles — PRIVATE data exposed during scoring. No local error.
discriminatingConstraint: Matching reads from the GLOBAL index. The GLOBAL record IS the enforcement layer.
keywords: Audience scoring reads xiigen-matching-profiles GLOBAL index written FLOW-02 xiigen-business-profiles PRIVATE Four public-safe

teachingPoint: A niche event with zero matching users is a valid successful promotion. audienceSize:0 is not an error. The event must exist for future matching when new users onboard.
positiveExample: NODE D gate passes with audienceSize:0. EventPromotionCompleted emits. DR-03-F.
negativeExample: audienceSize:0 → DataProcessResult.failure or gate blocks — prevents future discovery of the event.
discriminatingConstraint: Empty result is a valid state. No result is the only failure.
keywords: niche zero matching users valid successful promotion audienceSize error exist future onboard

teachingPoint: T62 emits 'PromotionCampaignCompleted' — not 'EventPromotionCompleted'. Two events, same trigger (EventCreated), different timelines, different consumers. Same name = duplicate signal to FLOW-08.
positiveExample: T62 triggers on EventCreated. Emits 'PromotionCampaignCompleted' after 7-day window.
negativeExample: T62 emits 'EventPromotionCompleted' — collision with NODE D. FLOW-08 receives duplicate bootstrap. No local error.
discriminatingConstraint: Time-decoupled processes share a trigger but must produce separate completion events.
keywords: PromotionCampaignCompleted EventPromotionCompleted events trigger EventCreated different timelines consumers name duplicate signal FLOW-08

teachingPoint: A3 reads the stored event's knowledgeScope before writing the index entry. Unconditional GLOBAL indexing makes private paid events searchable cross-tenant. No local error.
positiveExample: A3 reads event's stored knowledgeScope. GLOBAL events → GLOBAL index entry. PRIVATE events → PRIVATE index entry.
negativeExample: A3 unconditionally writes knowledgeScope:GLOBAL — private events leak cross-tenant via search.
discriminatingConstraint: Scope set at creation (A2) propagates to all derived records. Each derived-record writer reads source scope.
keywords: reads knowledgeScope writing index entry Unconditional GLOBAL indexing private paid events searchable

teachingPoint: A1 isPaidEvent check requires a payment config read from xiigen-payment-configs. If this read is absent, paid events are created without payment setup. Failure surfaces at payment time with no traceable connection to A1.
positiveExample: A1 CONNECTIONS: reads from xiigen-payment-configs when isPaidEvent=true. PAYMENT_CONFIG_MISSING if absent.
negativeExample: Validation-only node with no reads — isPaidEvent check silently passes on null config.
discriminatingConstraint: MACHINE checks that depend on external records must name the read source in CONNECTIONS.
keywords: isPaidEvent check payment config read xiigen-payment-configs absent paid events created without setup
